stories.headline: Trump threatens to seize Iran's Kharg Island as oil surges past $116 a barrel
stories.body: President Trump told the Financial Times he wants to "take the oil in Iran" and is considering seizing Kharg Island, the country's main oil export hub, saying it could be taken "very easily" but would require a sustained presence. The threat sent Brent crude surging past 116 dollars a barrel on Monday, its highest level in nearly two weeks, with Asian stock markets tumbling as Japan's Nikkei fell 3 percent and South Korea's Kospi dropped 3.4 percent. Brent is now up 59 percent since the start of March, on track for its biggest monthly gain ever, surpassing the 46 percent jump in September 1990 after Iraq invaded Kuwait. US Central Command confirmed that a further 3,500 sailors and marines have arrived in the Middle East aboard the USS Tripoli, bringing the total American military presence in the region to over 50,000 troops. Iran's parliament speaker warned that Iranian forces would "rain fire" on any US troops attempting to enter Iranian territory, and Tehran has reinforced defences on Kharg Island with additional air defences and anti-personnel mines.

stories.headline: Iranian attack on Kuwait power and desalination plant kills worker as war spreads to Gulf states
stories.body: An Iranian attack struck a power and water desalination plant in Kuwait on Monday, killing an Indian worker and causing significant damage to a service building, according to Kuwaiti authorities. Iran denied responsibility, claiming Israel was behind the attack. The incident came a day after Kuwait's defence ministry reported intercepting 14 missiles and 12 drones in its airspace, several targeting a military camp where 10 servicemen were injured. Qatar condemned the attack "in the strongest terms" and affirmed solidarity with Kuwait. The escalation highlights the growing threat to Gulf states' critical water infrastructure, as the region produces 40 percent of the world's desalinated water. Since the start of the US-Israeli war on Iran a month ago, more than 2,000 people have been killed in Iran and Iranian-aligned groups have struck back with drone and missile attacks targeting Israel and Gulf nations hosting US military assets.

stories.headline: Russia expels British diplomat over spying allegations in latest tit-for-tat
stories.body: Russia's Federal Security Service ordered the expulsion of a British diplomat, identified as second secretary Albertus Gerhardus Janse van Rensburg, accusing him of providing false information when entering the country and attempting to obtain sensitive economic intelligence during informal meetings with Russian experts. The diplomat has been given two weeks to leave. The UK Foreign Office called the accusations "completely unacceptable" and "complete nonsense," accusing Moscow of an "aggressive and co-ordinated campaign of harassment against British diplomats." The expulsion is the latest in a series of diplomatic tit-for-tat moves since Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022, with both countries regularly expelling each other's staff over spying allegations. The FSB warned Russian citizens to refrain from holding meetings with British diplomats to avoid "negative consequences, including criminal liability."

stories.headline: Myanmar's military chief Min Aung Hlaing nominated for presidency after sham elections
stories.body: Myanmar's military chief Min Aung Hlaing was nominated for the presidency as parliament convened on Monday, five years after he led the coup that overthrew the elected government of Aung San Suu Kyi. His selection is a foregone conclusion, as roughly 90 percent of parliamentary seats are held by loyalists, either serving military officers guaranteed a quarter of the seats or elected candidates from the military's own party. The general elections held between December and January were widely regarded as illegitimate, with major opposition parties banned from standing and large areas of the country excluded from voting due to the ongoing civil war. Under the constitution, Min Aung Hlaing must relinquish command of the armed forces as president, a risk given known discontent among some senior commanders. He has chosen staunch loyalist General Ye Win Oo as his replacement and created a new consultative council to maintain authority over both military and civilian affairs.

stories.headline: Thieves steal Renoir, Cezanne and Matisse paintings in three-minute heist at Italian museum
stories.body: Four masked men broke into the Magnani Rocca Foundation near Parma, Italy, on 22 March and stole three paintings worth an estimated nine million euros in under three minutes before fleeing over a fence. The stolen works are Les Poissons by Renoir, valued alone at six million euros, Still Life with Cherries by Cezanne, and Odalisque on the Terrace by Matisse. The gang forced their way through the main door of the Villa dei Capolavori and grabbed the paintings from the French Room on the first floor, only stopping when the alarm system went off. The foundation described the thieves as "structured and organised" and said they appeared to have intended to steal more works. News of the heist was only made public on Sunday, and the theft is now being investigated by Italy's Carabinieri and the Cultural Heritage Protection Unit of Bologna.

stories.headline: 'No Kings' protests draw millions across the US as police fire tear gas in Los Angeles
stories.body: The third wave of "No Kings" rallies drew an estimated eight million people to cities across the United States and in European capitals on Saturday, protesting the Iran war, immigration enforcement and the rising cost of living. In Los Angeles, police deployed tear gas near a federal detention centre and arrested dozens of demonstrators. Thousands filled Washington's National Mall and the steps of the Lincoln Memorial, while crowds shut down streets through New York's Times Square, where Robert De Niro told the BBC that Trump is "crazy" and that Americans "have to resist." In Minnesota, where two American citizens were killed by federal immigration agents in January, Bruce Springsteen performed his anti-immigration enforcement song "Streets of Minneapolis" outside the State Capitol. A White House spokesperson dismissed the protests as "Trump Derangement Therapy Sessions." The rallies coincided with growing public opposition to the war, with a generational divide over Iran emerging at the Conservative Political Action Conference.

stories.headline: Supreme Court to hear arguments on Trump's birthright citizenship order
stories.body: The Supreme Court will hear arguments on Wednesday over President Trump's executive order to deny US citizenship to children born in the country to parents who are in the US illegally or temporarily, a case that tests the meaning of the 14th Amendment's Citizenship Clause. Every lower court to consider the order has found it to be illegal and prevented it from taking effect. The administration's solicitor general compared the case to Brown v. Board of Education, arguing the court should correct "long-enduring misconceptions" about the Constitution, while opponents say the post-Civil War amendment's guarantee of citizenship to "all persons born or naturalized in the United States" has been settled law for over a century. The case is part of the administration's broader immigration crackdown and presents another test for a court that has allowed some anti-immigration efforts to proceed even after lower courts blocked them.

stories.headline: Airport chaos persists as shutdown hits 44 days despite Trump's emergency TSA pay order
stories.body: The partial government shutdown reached 44 days on Sunday, breaking the record set in November 2025, as airports continued to urge travellers to arrive hours early despite Trump's executive order on Friday directing the Department of Homeland Security to pay TSA agents immediately. White House border czar Tom Homan said TSA pay should begin early in the week but warned that ICE agents deployed to airports would remain "until the airports feel like they are 100 percent," maintaining a federal immigration enforcement presence at checkpoints that has drawn controversy. Congress left Washington for a two-week recess without a deal, with the Senate unable to pass a compromise bill and House Republicans insisting on full DHS funding including immigration enforcement. It remains unclear whether Trump's order to bypass Congress and pay TSA agents will face legal challenges, as the Constitution tasks Congress with authorising federal spending.

stories.headline: Arctic sea ice hits record low for winter as unprecedented heat shatters records worldwide
stories.body: Arctic sea ice shrank to tie its lowest measured level for the winter season, the National Snow and Ice Data Center announced, as extreme temperatures shattered March heat records across the United States, Mexico, Australia, Northern Africa and parts of Northern Europe. Sixteen US states broke March temperature records in the past week, with 27 locations recording temperatures that matched or exceeded their hottest April day on record. Climatologist Maximiliano Herrera called it "by far the most extreme heat event in world climatic history," noting that in Asia "dozens of thousands of monthly records" were smashed by margins of 17 to 19 degrees Celsius. The Arctic sea ice peaked at 14.29 million square kilometres, about 1.36 million square kilometres below the 1981-2010 average, an area roughly twice the size of Texas. Scientists warn that lower winter ice gives the summer melt season a head start, further reducing the ice that acts as Earth's refrigerator by reflecting sunlight.

stories.headline: Spain closes airspace to US military aircraft involved in Iran war
stories.body: Spain has closed its airspace to all US military aircraft participating in Operation Epic Fury, the US-Israeli campaign against Iran, widening its rift with Washington. Defence Minister Margarita Robles confirmed that Spain will not authorise the use of either the Rota and Moron de la Frontera military bases or its airspace for war-related operations, calling the conflict "profoundly illegal and profoundly unjust." The closure forces military planes based in the UK, France and elsewhere in Europe to bypass Spain en route to the Middle East, except in emergencies. Before the war began, Washington had sounded out Madrid about deploying B-52 and B-1 bombers at Spanish bases, but the request was declined. At least 15 KC-135 refuelling planes were diverted from Spanish bases to France and Germany at the end of February. Economy Minister Carlos Cuerpo said the decision had not affected commercial trade with the United States, despite Trump's earlier threats of economic reprisals.

stories.headline: Government and Catholic Church sign protocol for sex abuse reparations, with Ombudsman to decide compensation
stories.body: The Spanish government, the Conference of Bishops, the Confederation of Religious Orders and the Ombudsman signed a protocol on Monday establishing a mechanism for compensating victims of sexual abuse in the Catholic Church, including cases that have already prescribed under the statute of limitations. Under the agreement, which takes effect on 15 April, the Ombudsman will review each case individually and determine reparations, which may be economic, symbolic, restorative or spiritual, while the Church will foot the bill. No fixed scale of payments has been set because each case will be assessed on its merits. If the Church disputes the Ombudsman's proposal, a joint commission including victim associations will attempt to reach consensus, with the Ombudsman having the final word. About 100 people have so far been recognised as victims by the Church's own 2024 reparation scheme, of whom roughly 80 have received financial compensation. The protocol was negotiated with Vatican involvement.

stories.headline: Iran war hits Spanish mortgages as Euribor rises for the first time in two years
stories.body: The Euribor, the benchmark rate used to calculate most variable-rate mortgages in Spain, is set to close March at 2.53 percent, up 0.31 points from February and marking the first increase in mortgage costs in two years. The rise is driven by fears that the Iran war will reignite inflation through soaring energy prices, with the index briefly touching 3 percent in recent sessions. For a typical 180,000-euro mortgage over 30 years, the increase translates to an extra 12 euros per month or 144 euros per year. European Central Bank president Christine Lagarde recently signalled the bank's willingness to raise interest rates if the Middle East conflict affects price stability in the eurozone. Roughly 59 percent of outstanding mortgages in Spain are tied to variable or mixed rates, meaning any rise in the index translates almost immediately into higher monthly payments for millions of households.

stories.headline: Mazon asks to join Valencia flood disaster investigation as defendant
stories.body: Carlos Mazon, the president of the Valencia region, has asked the judge investigating the October 2024 DANA catastrophic floods to allow him to become a party to the case, saying he wants to see all the details of the investigation against him. Mazon's request comes as the judicial investigation continues into the government's delayed response to the floods that killed more than 220 people. The move is seen as a legal strategy to gain access to the full case file and prepare his defence ahead of any potential charges related to the emergency management failures.

stories.headline: Court orders enforcement of ruling striking down Catalan language decree for schools
stories.body: The Catalan High Court (TSJC) on Monday ordered the provisional enforcement of its September ruling that partially annulled the regional government's 2024 decree establishing Catalan as the sole vehicular language in schools. The decision, issued while schools are on Easter break, effectively suspends 11 articles that fixed Catalan and Aranese as the normal languages of instruction, administration, family communications and teaching materials. The Assembly for a Bilingual School in Catalonia, which brought the case, said it means any sanctions against teachers or students for using Spanish are now without legal basis. President Salvador Illa pledged to deploy "all political and legal resources necessary" to defend the Catalan school model, with his government announcing it would appeal. Junts accused the Illa government of being "complicit" in attacks on the language, ERC demanded a "response as a country," and the PP called for compliance rather than attempts to "circumvent" the ruling.

stories.headline: Groundforce launches indefinite strike at El Prat as ministry imposes 100 percent minimum services
stories.body: Ground handling workers at Barcelona-El Prat airport began an indefinite strike on Monday, the first working day of Semana Santa, after unions UGT, CCOO and USO called the action to protest breaches of the collective agreement and loss of purchasing power. However, the Transport Ministry imposed 100 percent minimum services, a decision unions called unconstitutional and which they plan to challenge before the Constitutional Court. Around 1,100 of the 6,000 Groundforce workers across 12 Spanish airports are based at Barcelona, with stoppages scheduled in three shifts throughout the day. Unions warned that the unprecedented minimum service levels effectively nullify the right to strike and could lead to covert work slowdowns causing flight delays. Groundforce, part of the Globalia group, handles airlines including Air Europa and Lufthansa. A separate strike by Menzies Group handling workers also hit airports in Bilbao and Palma over the weekend.

stories.headline: Police dismantle Turkish mafia network in Catalunya with 21 arrests across Europe
stories.body: The Mossos d'Esquadra and Spanish National Police, in coordination with Europol, dismantled a Turkish mafia network that was trafficking arms and drugs from Turkey into Catalunya and other parts of Spain, arresting 21 people across Catalonia, Andalusia and Bulgaria. The network's principal leader, classified by Europol as a High Value Target, was among three arrested in Sofia, while 14 were detained in Catalan municipalities including Barcelona, Mataro, Sabadell and Lloret de Mar. Officers seized seven semi-automatic pistols, one military-grade weapon, silencers, and 587 kilograms of marijuana and 76 kilograms of hashish with an estimated street value of 4.4 million euros. The investigation began in March 2025 after Mossos detected a rise in incidents involving illegally imported firearms from Turkey being used for threats, extortion and score-settling by criminal groups in Catalunya.

stories.headline: Barcelona approves controversial Thyssen Museum project on Passeig de Gracia
stories.body: Barcelona's city council gave final approval to the new Thyssen Museum project at the iconic corner of Passeig de Gracia and Gran Via, with votes from PSC, Junts, PP and Vox and opposition from Comuns and ERC. The project will transform the historic Palau Marcet, home to the legendary Cinema Comedia, into a museum designed by Barcelona's OUA studio and Anglo-German firm Casper Mueller Kneer. Plans include preserving and restoring the entire facade to its original colour, adding a new upper volume set back from the street for temporary exhibition halls, a terrace and a restaurant, bringing the building's height in line with surrounding structures on the intersection. The investment fund Stoneweg, which is backing the project, said criticism had been "a gift" that improved the final design, and insisted the goal is "to build a museum, not something else disguised as a museum."

stories.headline: Fire at Port of Barcelona sends black smoke column visible across the city
stories.body: A fire broke out at the Moll de Ponent area of the Port of Barcelona on Sunday, sending a large column of black smoke visible from across the city and surrounding municipalities. The blaze affected 20 semi-trailers near the Pont d'Europa that did not contain hazardous goods, according to city authorities. Ten fire crews responded along with a fireboat providing support from the water, and the fire was brought under control by around four in the afternoon with no injuries reported. The emergency number 112 received 76 calls related to the incident. The port resumed normal operations except in the affected area, where firefighters continued to douse and inspect the semi-trailers, while the B-10 highway experienced delays from onlookers slowing to watch.
